stem: What is the mean of {{a}}, {{a+d}}, and {{a+2*d}}?
solutionStandard: These three numbers are evenly spaced, so the mean is the middle value: \({{a+d}}\).

stem: What is the median of the data set \(4, 9, 2, 7, 5\)?
solutionStandard: Sorted: \(2,4,5,7,9\). The middle value is 5.

stem: A data set has minimum {{lo}} and maximum {{lo+r}}. What is the range?
What is the mode of the data set \(3, 7, 3, 9, 3, 5\)?
solutionStandard: 3 appears three times, more than any other value.

stem: The mean of {{n}} numbers is {{m}}. What is their sum?
solutionStandard: Sum \(=\) mean \(\times\) count \(={{m}}\times{{n}}={{n*m}}\).

stem: A fair six-sided die is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling a multiple of 3?
solutionStandard: Multiples of 3 on a die: 3 and 6, so \(P=2/6=1/3\).

stem: A store has {{a}} shirt styles and {{b}} pants styles. How many different shirt-and-pants outfits are possible?
solutionStandard: By the fundamental counting principle: \({{a}}\times{{b}}={{a*b}}\).

stem: Of 150 people surveyed, 30% prefer tea over coffee. How many people prefer tea?
In how many ways can {{n}} books be arranged in a row on a shelf?
solutionStandard: The number of arrangements is \(n!={{n}}!\).

stem: In a group of 60 people, 40 like at least one of two hobbies (hiking or painting). How many people like neither?
Group 1 has {{n1}} items averaging {{avg-k*n2}}. Group 2 has {{n2}} items averaging {{avg+k*n1}}. What is the average of all items combined?
solutionStandard: The deviations from {{avg}} are weighted so they cancel exactly: combined average \(={{avg}}\).

stem: A data set has a standard deviation of 6. If every value is increased by 10, what is the new standard deviation?
solutionStandard: Adding a constant to every value shifts the mean but does not change the spread, so the standard deviation stays 6.

stem: In how many ways can a president and a vice president be chosen from a group of {{n}} people?
solutionStandard: {{n}} choices for president, then {{n-1}} for vice president: \({{n}}\times{{n-1}}={{n*(n-1)}}\).

stem: In how many ways can a committee of 2 be chosen from a group of 8 people?
A fair {{n}}-sided die (faces numbered 1 to {{n}}) is rolled once. What is the probability, as a percent, that the result is even? (Enter your answer as a number, without the percent sign.)
solutionStandard: Exactly half of the numbers 1 through {{n}} are even (since {{n}} is even), so \(P=50\%\) regardless of the die's size.

stem: In a normal distribution, approximately what percent of the data falls within 1 standard deviation of the mean?
solutionStandard: By the empirical rule, about 68% of the data falls within 1 standard deviation of the mean.

stem: In a class of {{aOnly+bOnly+both+neither}} students, {{aOnly+both}} take Spanish and {{bOnly+both}} take French, with {{both}} taking both. How many students take at least one of the two languages?
solutionStandard: \(|S\cup F|=({{aOnly+both}})+({{bOnly+both}})-{{both}}={{aOnly+bOnly+both}}\).

stem: A pie chart shows a school's 400 students by grade: 25% freshmen. How many students are freshmen?
The mean of {{n}} numbers is {{m1}}. When one number is removed, the mean of the remaining {{n-1}} numbers is {{m1+d}}. What number was removed?
solutionStandard: Removed number \(=n\cdot m_1-(n-1)(m_1+d)={{m1-(n-1)*d}}\).

stem: A standard die is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling a 2 or a 5?
solutionStandard: These outcomes are mutually exclusive: \(P=1/6+1/6=2/6=1/3\).

stem: What is the median of {{a}}, {{a+d}}, {{a+2*d}}, {{a+3*d}}?
solutionStandard: With 4 evenly-spaced values, the median is the average of the two middle terms: \(\dfrac{({{a+d}})+({{a+2*d}})}{2}={{a+3*d/2}}\).

stem: A data set has a standard deviation of 4. If every value is doubled, what is the new standard deviation?
solutionStandard: Multiplying every value by a constant multiplies the standard deviation by that constant: \(4\times2=8\).

stem: In how many ways can 1st, 2nd, and 3rd place be awarded among {{n}} runners (no ties)?
A pizza shop offers 8 toppings. How many different 3-topping pizzas can be made (no repeats, order doesn't matter)?
Events A and B are independent, with \(P(A)={{pA/10}}\) and \(P(B)={{pB/10}}\). What is \(P(A \text{ and } B)\)?
solutionStandard: \(P(A\text{ and }B)=P(A)\times P(B)={{pA/10}}\times{{pB/10}}={{Math.round((pA/10)*(pB/10)*100)/100}}\).

stem: Scores are normally distributed with mean 500 and standard deviation 100. What percent of scores are below 400?
solutionStandard: 400 is 1 SD below the mean. About 34% falls between 400 and the mean, and 50% is below the mean overall, so below 400: \(50-34=16\%\).

stem: A company's profit was ${{v1}},000 in 2020 and ${{v1*(100+pct)/100}},000 in 2022. What was the percent increase?
solutionStandard: Percent increase \(=\dfrac{ {{v1*(100+pct)/100}} - {{v1}} }{ {{v1}} }\times100={{pct}}\%\).

stem: In a class, 24 students study biology, 18 study chemistry, and 10 study both. If every student studies at least one of the two subjects, how many students are in the class?
A charity raised ${{total}}. If {{pctA}}% goes to administrative costs, how much money, in dollars, goes directly to the cause?
solutionStandard: Directly to the cause: \((100-{{pctA}})\%\times{{total}}={{total*(100-pctA)/100}}\).

stem: A die is rolled twice. What is the probability of rolling at least one 6?
A data set of 9 distinct positive integers is strongly right-skewed (a long tail of large values).
solutionStandard: In a right-skewed distribution, a few unusually large values pull the mean above the median, since the mean is sensitive to outliers while the median is not.

stem: In how many ways can a committee of at least 1 person be chosen from a group of 4 people?
solutionStandard: Total subsets of 4 people is \(2^4=16\); excluding the empty subset (choosing no one) gives \(16-1=15\).

stem: In how many distinct ways can the letters of the word "BANANA" be arranged?
solutionStandard: "BANANA" has 6 letters with A repeated 3 times and N repeated 2 times: \(\dfrac{6!}{3!2!}=\dfrac{720}{12}=60\).

stem: A bag has red and blue marbles, with at least one of each color. Two marbles are drawn.
solutionStandard: Drawing without replacement removes a red marble before the second draw, always lowering the proportion of red marbles remaining (unless there's only one color). Quantity B is always at least as great, and strictly greater whenever both colors are present.

stem: A group has {{w}} women and {{m}} men. In how many ways can a team of 2 women and 2 men be chosen?
answerFormula: (function(){function C(n,k){let r=1;for(let i=0;i<k;i++)r*=(n-i);for(let i=1;i<=k;i++)r/=i;return Math.round(r);} return C(w,2)*C(m,2);})()
solutionStandard: Choose 2 women: \(C({{w}},2)\). Choose 2 men: \(C({{m}},2)\). Multiply the two counts.

stem: A store's sales total $60,000: Electronics is 30% of that. If Electronics sales increase by 20% (of their own value) while total sales stay the same, what percent of the new total do Electronics represent?
A bag has 4 red and 6 blue marbles. Two marbles are drawn without replacement. What is the probability that the two marbles are different colors? (Enter as a decimal.)
solutionStandard: \(P(\text{red then blue})=\frac{4}{10}\times\frac{6}{9}=\frac{24}{90}\). \(P(\text{blue then red})=\frac{6}{10}\times\frac{4}{9}=\frac{24}{90}\). Total: \(\frac{48}{90}=\frac{8}{15}\approx0.533\).

stem: A pie chart shows a company's ${{total}} million budget: R&D {{pctA}}%, Marketing {{pctB}}%, with the remaining {{100-pctA-pctB}}% split between Operations and Admin in an unspecified ratio.
solutionStandard: The combined Operations-and-Admin share is fixed, but with no information on how it splits between the two, either could be larger. The relationship cannot be determined.

stem: From {{n}} finalists, {{k}} distinct prizes (1st, 2nd, ..., {{k}}th place) are awarded, one to each of {{k}} different finalists. In how many ways can this be done?
solutionStandard: Since the prizes are distinct (order matters), this is \(P({{n}},{{k}})\): multiply {{k}} descending values starting from {{n}}.

stem: A normal distribution has mean 50 and standard deviation 5. What percent of the data falls between 40 and 60?
solutionStandard: 40 and 60 are exactly 2 SD below and above the mean, so about 95% of the data falls between them.

stem: Class A has average score \(p\) and Class B has average score \(q\), where \(p \neq q\). Class A has more students than Class B.
quantityA: The combined average of both classes
solutionStandard: The combined average always leans toward the larger class's average. If \(p>q\), the combined average exceeds the midpoint; if \(p<q\), it falls below. Since the relationship between \(p\) and \(q\) isn't given, the comparison cannot be determined.

stem: From {{n}} people, a group of 3 is chosen, and one of the 3 is then designated as leader. In how many ways can this be done?
answerFormula: (function(){function C(n,k){let r=1;for(let i=0;i<k;i++)r*=(n-i);for(let i=1;i<=k;i++)r/=i;return Math.round(r);} return C(n,3)*3;})()
solutionStandard: Choose the group of 3: \(C({{n}},3)\). Then choose 1 of those 3 as leader: multiply by 3.

stem: Three fair coins are flipped. What is the probability of getting at least 2 heads?
solutionStandard: Favorable outcomes (2 or 3 heads): HHT, HTH, THH, HHH — 4 out of 8 total outcomes: \(P=4/8=1/2\).

stem: A data set of {{n}} numbers has a mean of {{m}}. One more number is added, making the mean of all {{n+1}} numbers equal to {{m+e}}. What number was added?
solutionStandard: Added number \(=(n+1)(m+e)-nm=m+(n+1)e={{m+(n+1)*e}}\).

stem: \(P(\text{likes math}) = 0.7\) and \(P(\text{likes science}) = 0.6\) in a class. \(P(\text{likes both})\) is not given.
solutionStandard: \(P(\text{math or science})=1.3-P(\text{both})\). Since \(P(\text{both})\) can range from 0.3 up to 0.6, the union ranges from 0.7 up to exactly 1 — it could equal 1 or be less. The relationship cannot be determined.

stem: How many diagonals does a nonagon (9 sides) have?
solutionStandard: Segments between any 2 of the 9 vertices: \(C(9,2)=36\). Subtract the 9 sides: \(36-9=27\).

stem: {{n}} people, including a couple who insist on sitting next to each other, are seated in a row. How many seating arrangements are possible?
solutionStandard: Treat the couple as one block: \(({{n}}-1)!\) ways to arrange the units, times \(2!\) for the couple's internal order.

stem: In a group of 100 people, 60 like jazz and 45 like rock. The number who like both genres is not given.
quantityA: The number of people who like exactly one of the two genres
solutionStandard: If \(x\) like both, exactly one \(=105-2x\). Since \(x\) can range from 5 to 45, exactly-one ranges from 15 to 95 — sometimes less than 45, sometimes more, sometimes equal. Cannot be determined.

stem: Distribution X has mean 500 and standard deviation 50. Distribution Y has mean 500 and standard deviation 80. Both are normal.
quantityA: The percent of Distribution X's data above 600
quantityB: The percent of Distribution Y's data above 600
solutionStandard: For X, 600 is 2 SD above the mean (a relatively far tail). For Y, 600 is only 1.25 SD above the mean, much closer to the bulk of the data, so more than X's tail lies beyond it. Quantity B is greater.

stem: Events A, B, and C are mutually independent, with \(P(A)={{pA/10}}\), \(P(B)={{pB/10}}\), and \(P(C)={{pC/10}}\). What is \(P(A \text{ and } B \text{ and } C)\)?
solutionStandard: For independent events, multiply: \({{pA/10}}\times{{pB/10}}\times{{pC/10}}={{Math.round((pA/10)*(pB/10)*(pC/10)*1000)/1000}}\).
